caption.text: This project invites you to step back in time and discover the community that once existed here before the construction of public housing.
caption.title: Welcome to Orchard Park!

caption.text: Established in 1935, the Boston Housing Authority (BHA) quickly embraced public housing as a solution to the city’s housing problems. In the years before World War II, it completed eight major developments: one funded through the Public Works Administration and seven through the U.S. Housing Authority.
caption.title: Introduction

caption.text: Orchard Park (labeled 2-5 on the map), built in Roxbury, was the sixth of these projects, with its first tenants moving in between 1942 and 1944.

The physical layout of Orchard Park reflected the planning ideals of its time. Designed as a modern public housing development, it replaced the dense street grid of the earlier neighborhood with a more open and controlled arrangement of space.

Originally, the project contained 774 units arranged on a block site, and later reduced to 711, organized into multiple three-story apartment buildings across large, landscaped blocks, often described as a “superblock.”

Streets were reconfigured into cul-de-sacs, and internal pathways connected buildings through shared green spaces. In general, large blocks, open lawns, and trees are all designed to create a sense of order and security in this residential environment.

Like other public housing developments in Boston, Orchard Park was racially managed from the outset. Although it was not officially designated as a segregated project, the Boston Housing Authority informally controlled where residents could live within the development. Black families were confined to a small cluster of four adjacent buildings, while the majority of the apartments were reserved for white tenants.

Nevertheless, building this modern housing project required the complete removal of what came before. Developing the 774-unit Orchard Park complex meant demolishing hundreds of homes and businesses and displacing over a thousand residents.

The photos capture the scenes of the neighborhood at the time. Rather than a landscape of decay, many of the buildings and stores appeared to be well-structured and remained in regular use. Despite this, officials justified the clearance by labeling the area as “blighted,” arguing that its physical conditions were detrimental to public health, safety, and moral standards.

For residents, however, the process was more complicated and contentious. Not all families left willingly. Some of them refused to leave and were forcibly evicted, while the uncertainty of transition led to unrest, including repeated fires that required constant police patrols. What was presented by officials as a necessary step toward progress, for those who lived there, was a moment of disruption and loss. Homes, businesses, and social networks were dismantled in a relatively short period of time, leaving behind the loss of an existing community.

Therefore, this project seeks to revisit what Orchard Park looked like before the construction of public housing, as a way of commemorating the neighborhood that once existed on this site. Combining newspaper accounts, historical photographs, and maps, these materials offer a vivid picture of a community shaped by everyday life. In revisiting these fragments, this project invites you to think about what this area gained but also what it ultimately lost, through the processes of redevelopment.
